text: This study presents a sustainable and cost-effective green synthesis of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) utilizing polyphenol-rich extracts from *Phoenix dactylifera L.* (date palm) leaves as an eco-friendly reducing and stabilizing agent. To optimize the synthesis, the influence of varying concentration ratios between the zinc precursor and the plant extract was systematically investigated. Structural and morphological characterization through X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirmed the formation of crystalline ZnO-NPs with a hexagonal structure. The resulting nanoparticles exhibited diverse morphologies—including spherical, cubic, and hexagonal nanorods—with average sizes ranging from 18.1 to 61.6 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy identified the presence of phytochemical functional groups on the NP surfaces, suggesting their role in the reduction and capping process. Functional assessments revealed significant antioxidant properties, determined by DPPH radical scavenging and total antioxidant capacity (TAC) assays. Furthermore, the green-synthesized ZnO-NPs demonstrated potent antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive (*Staphylococcus aureus*) and Gram-negative (*Escherichia coli*) bacteria. These findings highlight the potential of using date palm leaf extracts as a viable, non-toxic alternative to conventional chemical methods for producing functional nanomaterials suitable for biomedical and pharmaceutical applications.
